statement.markdown: You are given a recursive function that is intended to traverse a directory tree and print the names of all files found within it. However, the function contains a subtle logical bug that prevents it from working correctly.
statement.inputFormat: A directory path as a string
statement.outputFormat: A string containing the names of all files found in the directory tree
statement.notes: The function should traverse the directory tree recursively, printing the names of all files it encounters.

constraints.description: The maximum number of files in a directory

examples.explanation: The function should print the names of all files in the directory tree, including files in subfolders

hints.text: Pay attention to the base case in the recursive function

editorial.title: Fixing the Recursive Directory Walker
editorial.intuition: To fix the function, you need to correctly implement the base case to handle the situation when a directory has no subdirectories.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve using a recursive function to traverse the directory tree, but this would be inefficient and prone to errors.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a recursive function that checks if a directory has subdirectories before attempting to traverse them.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a recursive function with a base case that checks if a directory has subdirectories before attempting to traverse them.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that it correctly handles the base case and recursively traverses the directory tree.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution would involve manually tracing the execution of the function to ensure it correctly prints the names of all files in the directory tree.
commonMistakes: Failing to implement the base case correctly
Not checking for subdirectories before traversing a directory
